coredata.dc:title: B-doped graphene quantum dots array as fluorescent sensor platforms for plasticizers detection
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xiaofang
Deng, Jiaxi
Li, Jiawei
Dong, Jiangbo
Liu, Huan
Zhao, Jinsong
Luo, Xiaogang
Huo, Danqun
Hou, Changjun
coredata.dc:description: Human health and environmental safety are imperceptibly disturbed by illegally used plasticizers. A fluorescence sensor array based on B-doped graphene quantum dots was designed to detect six plasticizers. Specifically, the dexterous design is the first to disperse B-doped graphene quantum dots in two solvents of different polarities (N,N-Dimethylformamide and Cyclohexane), select three characteristic fluorescence peaks of B-doped graphene quantum dots in the above two solvents, and construct a 3 × 2 array, with a total of 6 array points. The basis of the detection is that the plasticizers affect the dispersion of B-doped graphene quantum dots in different solutions, which are accompanied by the change of its fluorescence signals. Or the plasticizers affect the electron transfer process of B-doped graphene quantum dots in different solvents, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Fluorescence information output by the sensor array showed a good linear relationship with the concentrations of the six plasticizers. The quantitative detection capability can be specified as: the detection range is 100 ng/mL-195 mg/mL, and the detection limit is 1.05–11.58 ng/mL. The fluorescence sensor array also showed good analytical performance in real samples, with recoveries ranging from 95% to 106%. This method has satisfactory specificity, reproducibility, stability and practicality, making it potential for application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Plasticizer
Fluorescence sensor array
Graphene quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Verification of fluorescence sensor array performance
Mechanism of fluorescence sensor array construction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the construction of the fluorescence sensor array.
Fluorescence spectrum of B-GQDs suspensions in DMF and CH.
Characterization of B-GQDs. (a-b) TEM micrographs of B-GQDs; (c) FT-IR spectra of B-GQDs; (d) Survey-scan XPS spectra of B-GQDs; XPS spectra of B-GQDs: (e) O1s, (f) N1s, (g) C1s, and (h) B1s.
Responses of series concentrations of plasticizer-5 with B-GQDs dispersed in (a) DMF and (b) CH; Selectivity of B-GQDs dispersed in (c) DMF and (d) CH to plasticizer-5.
Quantitative detection of six plasticizers from array points 1–6. (a) plasticizer-6; (b) plasticizer-5; (c) plasticizer-4; (d) plasticizer-2; (e) plasticizer-3; (f) plasticizer-1.

Quantitative detection of fluorescence intensity graph of six plasticizer.(a) Plasticizer 6, array point 1; (b) Plasticizer 5, array point 2; (c) Plasticizer 4, array point 3; (d) Plasticizer 2, array point 4; (e) Plasticizer 3, array point 5; (f) Plasticizer 1, array point 6.
Specificity of fluorescent sensor array. (a) array point-1; (b) array point-2; (c) array point-3; (d) array point-4; (e) array point-5; (f) array point-6.
Stability and lifetime of the fluorescent sensor array. (a) array point-1; (b) array point-2; (c) array point-3; (d) array point-4; (e) array point-5; (f) array point-6.

row.entry: DIBP, DEHP, DBP, DAP, DINP, DIDP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental results of plasticizer-5 labeling recovery.

head.ce:title: B-doped graphene quantum dots array as fluorescent sensor platforms for plasticizers detection

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors made equal contrition to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Human health and environmental safety are imperceptibly disturbed by illegally used plasticizers. A fluorescence sensor array based on B-doped graphene quantum dots was designed to detect six plasticizers. Specifically, the dexterous design is the first to disperse B-doped graphene quantum dots in two solvents of different polarities (N,N-Dimethylformamide and Cyclohexane), select three characteristic fluorescence peaks of B-doped graphene quantum dots in the above two solvents, and construct a 3 × 2 array, with a total of 6 array points. The basis of the detection is that the plasticizers affect the dispersion of B-doped graphene quantum dots in different solutions, which are accompanied by the change of its fluorescence signals. Or the plasticizers affect the electron transfer process of B-doped graphene quantum dots in different solvents, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Fluorescence information output by the sensor array showed a good linear relationship with the concentrations of the six plasticizers. The quantitative detection capability can be specified as: the detection range is 100 ng/mL-195 mg/mL, and the detection limit is 1.05–11.58 ng/mL. The fluorescence sensor array also showed good analytical performance in real samples, with recoveries ranging from 95% to 106%. This method has satisfactory specificity, reproducibility, stability and practicality, making it potential for application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Phthalic acid esters (PAEs) are good helpers to enhance the performance of plastics, rubber and other products [1]. Therefore, PAEs are mass-produced in industry and are frequently used in various consumer products [2]. The wanton increase and ubiquity of PAEs are threatening human health and the public environment step by step. PAEs have estrogen-like effects, hepatotoxicity, nephrotoxicity, may cause cellular metabolic disorders, cardiovascular system imbalances, etc. They ultimately have negative effects on humans. PAEs are human killers who hide in the shadows [3,4], so finding a fast and sensitive detection method is urgent.

The detection methods of PAEs are commonly used high performance liquid chromatography [5,6], high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [7], gas chromatography, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [8,9], infrared spectroscopy [10], surface-enhanced raman scattering spectroscopy [11–13], etc. Although the above methods are highly accurate and reproducible, they are usually large instruments, expensive and complicated to operate. More specifically, the matrix effect in high performance liquid chromatography and gas chromatography separation, and the co-eluting substances will change the ionization efficiency of the component to be tested, which will cause signal disturbance. Compared with conventional and traditional detection methods, infrared spectroscopy has no obvious advantage in detection sensitivity, and cannot be applied to the detection of water-bearing samples. Surface-enhanced raman scattering spectroscopy uses metal nanomaterials such as Au and Ag as the substrate. Under the excitation of light, local surface plasmon resonance (hot spot) will be generated. When the substance to be tested is placed in the hot spot, a greatly enhanced raman signal of the substance to be detected will be obtained. The excellent performance of surface-enhanced raman scattering spectroscopy with high sensitivity cannot cover the drawbacks of being susceptible to interference and contamination. More directly, the resulting spectrums are complex and inconvenient for effective analysis. This requires extracting valid data from the accumulated information, which is time-consuming and labor-intensive.

Under the background of flourishing chemical sensing technology, there are many kinds of available sensors. Among them, the fluorescence sensor, as an optical sensor, is an important detection tool, which can achieve fast and specific targeting of the target [14,15]. It often has high sensitivity, strong anti-interference, and it can cope with complex detection environments. Currently, fluorescent sensors combined with array are the icing on the cake. The fluorescence sensor array consists of multiple sensing units [16,17]. Each unit exists independently and responds uniquely to different substances. It can avoid the dependence and fatigue of the test object of a single sensor, thereby broadening the detection range. Multi-signal, high-dimensional fluorescence sensor array technology can improve the detection performance of similar substances [18,19]. It is forward-looking to apply highly luminescent fluorescent nanomaterials in fluorescent sensor array. Highly luminescent fluorescent nanomaterials are the accelerators for fluorescent sensor array to drive towards a better path. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a favored zero-dimensional nanomaterial [20]. Its three dimensions are all in the scale range of 1–100 nm. Surface functionalization and doping GQDs with heteroelements can tune their electronic and optical properties to enhance the fluorescence quantum yield [21]. Commonly doped elements are N, B, S, etc [22–26]. GQDs are luminescence-stable and friendly materials. The excellent properties of GQDs make them shine in the field of fluorescence sensor array. The enhancement or quenching of its fluorescence intensity can be used to specifically identify some substances, such as anti-cancer drugs [27,28] and water pollutants [29].

In this work, B-doped graphene quantum dots (B-GQDs) were synthesized by one-pot solvothermal method using 1,3,6-trinitropyren and boric acid. Two different polar solutions of N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and Cyclohexane (CH) were used as solvents for B-GQDs, and combined with the three characteristic fluorescence emission peaks of B-GQDs themselves. So, a 3 × 2 fluorescence sensor array was constructed. The detection steps of the fluorescence sensor array are simple. It requires low experimental operation. After dissolving the samples containing plasticizers with DMF and CH, only an extremely small amount of B-GQDs can be introduced to realize the multi-dimensional detection of plasticizers at six array points. We found that plasticizers specifically quenched the fluorescence of B-GQDs. Taking advantage of this property, effective detection of plasticizer 1–6 was carried out. After the fluorescence intensity values were extracted and processed, it was found that there was a linear relationship with the concentration of the six plasticizers. Compared with the existing plasticizer detection methods, we report a new method that is simple, efficient, low-cost, and highly sensitive.
See Tables S1 and S2 for reagent and equipment information.

In particular, plasticizer 1 is diisobutyl phthalate (DIBP), plasticizer 2 is di(2-ethylhexyl)phthalate (DEHP), plasticizer 3 is dibutyl phthalate (DBP), plasticizer 4 is diallyl phthalate (DAP), plasticizer 5 is diisononyl phthalate (DINP), plasticizer 6 is diisodecyl phthalate (DIDP).

Referring to the previous method [30]: first, 2 g of pyrene was dissolved in 16 mol/L, 240 mL of concentrated nitric acid, stirred continuously at 80 °C, and nitrified for 18 h. To securely modify the nitro group, the mixture was then cooled to 25 °C and diluted with 2 L deionized water. After the above dilution was stirred for 24 h, it was filtered and dried at 60 °C for 24 h. This step yields 1,3,6-trinitropyrene.

2 g 1,3,6-trinitropyren and 1 g boric acid were uniformly dispersed in 50 mL DMF, transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave, and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. The reacted liquid, namely B-GQDs, was passed through a 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove impurities. The B-GQDs stock solution was stored at 4 °C.

5 μL of B-GQDs stock solution was diluted to 10 mL with different solvents (DMF, CH) and formulated as B-GQDs prep solution. 100 μL of plasticizer-containing samples (or interference/real samples) were respectively diluted to 1 mL with DMF and CH, formulated into two sample prep solutions. The fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence characteristic peaks can be obtained by mixing 20 μL of B-GQDs prep solution and 400 μL of sample prep solutions (solvent is DMF) under 350 nm excitation. The fluorescence data at 391, 413 and 434 nm were used as array points 1, 2 and 3. Again, only sample prep solutions (solvent is DMF) were replaced with sample prep solutions (solvent is CH). The fluorescence data at 386, 408 and 430 nm were subsequently obtained for array sites 4, 5 and 6.

The Δfluorescence intensity (ΔF) is the response value of this fluorescence sensing array (ΔF =｜F before reaction - F after reaction｜). All experiments were repeated four times. The mean and error bars (standard deviation) were then calculated.

Plasticizer 5 has more isomers, and it is a difficult object for sensing array detection, so it was chosen as the object of feasibility verification. Five concentrations of plasticizer 5 (0, 0.3, 1.6, 8.0, 40.0 mg/mL) were used to test the responsiveness of this sensor array. Later, the solutions to be detected (plasticizer-5, tetrahydrofuran, dichloromethane, ethyl acetate, and styrene) were prepared with DMF and CH at a concentration of 15.63 mg/mL. The detection procedures were followed to verify the practical feasibility of this work.

Plasticizers 1–6 were selected for detecting. They were all formulated with DMF and CH to series concentrations ranging from 100 ng/mL-200 mg/mL. Data from six array points were obtained simultaneously following the detection procedures. Then, the data from each array point were linearly fitted and the array point with the strongest correlation of the fitted results was selected as the dominant point for the detection of this plasticizer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of fluorescence sensor array performance
ce:section.ce:para: Specificity, reproducibility, and stability are the criteria for evaluating sensor array performance. Plasticizer-5 (39.06 mg/mL) was chosen for the reproducibility experiment. In addition, plasticizer-5 was also subjected to a 20-day stability test. Then, the same concentration of 6 plasticizers (1.563 mg/mL) and 10 times higher concentration (15.63 mg/mL) of interfering substances were subjected to specificity experiment. Interfering substances are tetrahydrofuran, benzyl alcohol, tert-butyl alcohol, n-butanol, methanol, ethyl valerate, heptane, 1-hexanol, n-hexane, butyraldehyde, benzene, trichloroethane, phosphoric acid, 3-methyl-1-butanol, isopropyl alcohol, 1,2,4-trimethylbenzene, ethyl butyrate, ethyl silicate, and ethyl hexanoate. The above operations are performed in accordance with the detection procedures.

Two detection methods were used for the analysis of real samples: this fluorescence sensor array and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS). The procedure using this fluorescence sensor array is described in "1.3 Detection procedures and data acquisition".

Test parameters of GC-MS: the 0.75 g sample to be tested was taken in the sample tube. A 400 mm × 25 mm glass column with 1:1 mobile phase of ethyl acetate and cyclohexane was used at a flow rate of 5 mL/min. The effluent collection time was 16–25 min. The column was a HP-5MS capillary column with an inlet dimension of 260 °C and a non-split injection. Then, the initial temperature was 60 ℃, heated to 220 ℃ at a rate of 20 ℃/min and held for 1 min, then heated to 250 ℃ (5 ℃/min) and held for 1 min, and finally heated to 290 ℃ (20 ℃/min) and held for 7.5 min

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence sensor array construction
ce:section.ce:para: One-pot solvothermal method was chosen to synthesize B-GQDs with high fluorescence intensity. Six array points were constructed based on the abundance of fluorescence characteristic peaks and solvents. The sensor array can achieve effective detection of six plasticizers ( Fig. 1).

It may be due to the interaction between B and graphene quantum dots. B-GQDs and GQDs at the same concentration, the former has a stronger fluorescence performance (Fig. S1). Therefore, we doped B in GQDs, which can provide a wide response range for sensing detection. Suspensions of B-GQDs in DMF or CH have three characteristic emission regions ( Fig. 2). They are specifically divided into: (a) 350–400 nm, π→π * transition of CC, (b) 400–430 nm, n→π * transition of CO and CN, (c) 430–460 nm, n→π * transitions of non-bonding electrons such as B-C, C-O, B-O, N-B, C-NO2.

In the application of organic dyes, the phenomenon of "solvatochromism" is common [31–33]. Similar to the above phenomenon, B-GQDs are solvent-dependent [30,34]. DMF is referred to be a "universal solvent" due to its exceptional performance to solubilize with the majority of organic and inorganic compounds. And the relative polarity is 0.386. CH is a saturated compound that does not readily react with other substances at room temperature. The polarity of CH is very different from that of DMF, and its relative polarity is 0.006. DMF and CH were chosen as solvents because they have different polarities and saturation levels. Most importantly, the dispersion ability of B-GQDs in the two solvents resulted in different fluorescence signals. Under ultraviolet light, the suspension of B-GQDs in DMF appeared blue-violet, while that in CH appeared bright yellow. DMF may have a strong interaction with -NO2 existing in B-GQDs, resulting in a red shift and increased fluorescence intensity. Benefit from the fact that B-GQDs have three characteristic emission peaks of fluorescence. And its solvent dependence leads to a shift in the characteristic peak positions. 391, 413, and 434 nm were selected as array points 1, 2, and 3 in sequence (DMF as solvent). 386, 408, and 430 nm are regarded as array points 4, 5, and 6, respectively (CH as solvent).

The suspensions in DMF and CH exhibited good dispersibility, which in turn directly affected the fluorescence properties [35,36]. Solvent molecules with different physicochemical properties change the interfacial structure (such as local defects and chemical bonding) between the surfaces of B-GQDs. When the analyte is reintroduced, the interface structure is changed again, and the dispersion reaches a new equilibrium. In addition, there are other factors in the analyte that affect the fluorescence properties of B-GQDs. For example, the carbonyl group in the plasticizer has an electron-withdrawing effect. It can accept electrons from the excited state to generate photo-induced electron transfer and cause fluorescence quenching.

The detection principle of the fluorescence sensor array can be briefly summarized as: the analyte, the solvent and the B-GQDs interact to form a polymorphic complex. The composite of the three changes their interfacial structure, which ultimately affects the dispersion of B-GQDs. Also, the groups contained in the analyte may affect the electron transfer process of B-GQDs. In conclusion, dispersion and electron transfer affect the fluorescence properties of B-GQDs, and the diverse fluorescence signals are the passcodes to complete the detection.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to observe the morphology of B-GQDs. Fig. 3(a-b) shows that the as-prepared B-GQDs are uniformly dispersed and spherical as a whole. Its particle size distribution map (see Fig. S2) shows a normal distribution centered at 2–4 nm, with an average particle size of 3 nm. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) was used to confirm the functional groups attached to B-GQDs. From Fig. 3(c), the sample contains carboxyl groups because C-O stretching in the carboxyl groups is found at 1000–1200 cm−1. The peak corresponding to 1650 cm also−1 shows the existence of CC and CN. Crucially, 1080 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1 correspond to the stretching of C-B and B-O, respectively. This confirms that the GQDs are successfully doped with B. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) can provide information such as elemental composition and content, molecular structure, chemical bonds, etc. Fig. 3(d) is the survey-scan XPS spectrum of B-GQDs. C1s (284.21 eV), N1s (405.21 eV), O1s (532.07 eV), B1s (188.03 eV) are the four main elements it has, and their atomic contents are 72.95%, 15.41%, 8.16%, 3.49% in turn. Fig. 3(e-h) are high-resolution O1s, N1s, C1s and B1s peaks of B-GQDs. The peaks of the O1s spectrum are composed of CO and C-O/B-O/C-NO2. It is worth mentioning that the C-NO2 is one of the factors affecting the dispersion performance of B-GQDs. The N1s spectrum consists of the following components: N-B, graphitic N, pyridimic/pyrrolic N, and NO2, corresponding to 398.82 eV, 401.46 eV, 403.88 eV, and 405.47 eV. According to the bond type of C atom, the C1s spectrum can be decomposed into four peaks: C-O (285.3 eV), CO/C-C (284.5 eV), C-B (283.8 eV). The presence of the C-B group indicates the presence of B doping in the graphite lattice. And, the peak of B1s is supported by B-N (187.8 eV), B-C (188.8 eV), BC2O (189.3 eV), BCO2 (190.5 eV). From this, it can also be inferred that the B atoms were successfully doped into the backbone of the graphene quantum dots. And the above characterization results are compared with the references [30], which confirm the successful preparation of B-GQDs.

Plasticizer-5 can quench the fluorescence of B-GQDs dispersed in DMF and CH. At the same time, B-GQDs fluorescence is quenched, which is selective. Fig. 4(a) is a fluorescence spectrum obtained by using DMF as a solvent. Fig. 4(b) shows that CH is the solvent. They show a concomitant decrease in the quenching ability of plasticizer-5 from higher to lower concentrations. This lays the foundation for subsequent quantitative detection. In Fig. 4(c-d), the fluorescence intensity of B-GQDs was hardly affected in the face of the four interferents (tetrahydrofuran, dichloromethane, ethylacetate and styrene). This indicates that the effect of plasticizer on B-GQDs is specific. The above proves the feasibility of this research.

The detection ability of the fluorescence sensor array was evaluated in the range of 100 ng/mL-195 mg/mL. Six plasticizers can be quantified for each array point, and the corresponding fingerprint maps and fluorescence spectra were established (Figs. S3 and S4). But we only list the optimal array point for one plasticizer in Table S3 and Fig. 5, their corresponding fluorescence spectra are shown in Fig. 6. It should be emphasized that the optimal array point is chosen based on the plasticizer's strongest association with that array point's response. Specifically, the detection range for plasticizer-4 was 100 ng/mL-313 μg/mL. The linear equation was Y= 193.8lg10 X-201.07, and the R2 was 0.9819. Fortunately, for these six plasticizers, the lowest limit of detection (LOD) was 1.05 ng/mL and the highest LOD was 11.58 ng/mL. This is sufficient to detect the maximum allowable concentration of plasticizers by governments or international agencies.

Herein, Table 1 compares the fluorescence sensor array in this work with other detection methods for plasticizers. The fluorescence sensing array established in this study has a wide detection range and a low detection limit. It does not have to worry about the cost pressure and susceptibility of expensive antigen-antibody. It also does not require the tedious process of precisely preparing template molecules and modifying electrodes layer by layer. Convenience, low-cost, and high-sensitivity are its advantages.

Specificity, reproducibility and stability are important indicators to measure the good performance of sensing array. Considering the solvent dependence of B-GQDs, there may be some interfering substances in the routine detecting of plasticizers. As shown in Fig. 7, we selected more than 20 interfering substances to explore the fluorescence sensor array. The concentrations of these interfering substances are all 10 times that of plasticizers. Taking array point-1 as an example, the response values of the plasticizers and the fluorescent sensing array are 4.8–57 times that of the interfering substance. This verifies that the fluorescence sensing array has excellent specificity in detection.

Under the same conditions, three sets of fluorescent sensor arrays were used to repeatedly detect 39.06 ng/mL plasticizer-5. From Fig. S5, the relative standard deviations (RSD) of the fluorescence intensities are 0.84%, 0.97%, 0.50%, 0.51%, 0.42%, and 0.53%, which correspond to array point 1–6 one-to-one. So, this fluorescence sensor array has good reproducibility.

The stability and lifetime of the fluorescence sensor array were further explored through 20 days of continuous testing ( Fig. 8). Fluorescence values were summarized for all array points after response: at day 7, the fluorescence intensity decayed to 90% of the initial, at day 13, to 80% of the initial, and at day 20, to 70% of the initial. Overall, the stability of this fluorescence sensor array is good. To guarantee the correctness and reliability of the detection results, the lifetime of this fluorescence sensing array is about 13 days.

As an important chemical product additive, plasticizers are widely found in food packaging, cosmetics, medical equipment and the environment. Excessive exposure to the environment with excessive plasticizers will bring chronic harm to the human reproductive and immune systems. Therefore, real sample analysis is a necessary means to investigate the detection method. Since the COVID-19 outbreak, medical disinfectant alcohol (MDA) has become a must-have for people. MDA packaging containers are generally plastic. The plasticizers are very likely to penetrate into the MDA, which will provide the plasticizers with the opportunity of direct contact with human skin.

Actual sample detecting was pursued to further evaluate practicality. Therefore, MDA was selected as the real sample, and the concentration of plasticizer 1–6 was determined by the standard addition method. Plasticizer 5 has a larger molecular weight and more isomers, which can easily interfere with the sensor response results, so it was chosen as a special case for the real sample analysis. Specifically, the retention time of plasticizer 5 was 19.526 min. It has qualitative ions of 77, 133, 163 and 194 (m/z) and quantitative ion of 163 (m/z). Table 2 presents the recoveries of Plasticizer 5 in the range of 95%− 106% with two detection methods (this work and GC-MS), and the RSD were 1%− 9%. The test results for all plasticizers can be found in Table S3. The above indicates that this sensor array has a good application prospect for the detection of plasticizer.

In view of the specific quenching effect of plasticizers in B-GQDs suspension (DMF and CH as solvent), the performance of this fluorescence sensor array was gradually developed and explored. The detection range of 6 plasticizers is 100 ng/mL-195 mg/mL, and the LOD can be as low as 1.05 ng/mL. The fluorescence sensor array is reproducible, which provides strong support for the smoothness of experiment. It also shows good specificity in more than 20 interfering substances, and the stability is also maintained well. In addition, it also had good strength in the standard recovery process of real samples. Recovery rates ranged from 95% to 106%. This method is expected to be applied to the detection of plasticizer in daily necessities, food and the environment. But it still needs to move towards miniaturization and intelligence.

Changjun Hou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Danqun Huo: Conceptualization, Methodology. Xiaogang Luo: Conceptualization, Methodology. Xiaofang Liu: Data analysis, Manuscript writing. Jiaxi Deng: Experimental operation. Jiawei Li: Editing manuscript reviewing and editing, Constructive analysis. Jiangbo Dong: Constructive analysis. Huan Liu: Constructive analysis. Jinsong Zhao: Constructive analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 31171684), Graduate Scientific Research and Innovation Foundation of Chongqing, China (Grant CYS22130), Special project for Performance Incentive and Guidance of Chongqing Scientific Research Institutions (cstc2020jxjl120007), Sichuan Science and Technology Program (21ZYZFZDYF0019), Brew Microorganisms Technology and Application of Key Laboratory Project in Sichuan Province (NJ2020–03), Chongqing Graduate Tutor Team Construction Project, Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University for TEM/XPS and the sharing fund of Chongqing University's large equipment.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132989.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microplastics: A major source of phthalate esters in aquatic environments
J. Hazard Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Cao, H. Lin, K. Zhang, S. Xu, M. Yan, K.M.Y. Leung, et al., Microplastics: A major source of phthalate esters in aquatic environments, J Hazard Mater, 432(2022) 128731.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phthalates in dormitory dust and human urine: A study of exposure characteristics and risk assessments of university students
Sci. Total Environ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Hua, S. Guo, J. Xu, X. Yang, H. Zhu, Y. Yao, et al., Phthalates in dormitory dust and human urine: A study of exposure characteristics and risk assessments of university students, Sci Total Environ, (2022) 157251.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review on occurrence, sources of contamination, and mitigation strategies of phthalates in vegetable oils
Eur. J. Lipid Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Y. Wang, M.Q. Wang, E.Q. Yang, X.M. Chen, F.G. Pan, Review on Occurrence, Sources of Contamination, and Mitigation Strategies of Phthalates in Vegetable Oils, European Journal of Lipid Science and Technology, 124(2022).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in adsorbents for the removal of phthalate esters from water: Material, modification, and application
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ye, B. Zhao, Y. Zhou, J. Du, M. Huang, Recent advances in adsorbents for the removal of phthalate esters from water: Material, modification, and application, Chemical Engineering Journal, 409(2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ionic liquid enhanced magnetic solid-phase extraction of phthalate esters in food samples with oleic acid coated-Fe3O4 nanoparticles prior to HPLC
Sep. Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Liu, Y. Liu, C. Cheng, Y. Yang, Ionic liquid enhanced magnetic solid-phase extraction of phthalate esters in food samples with oleic acid coated-Fe3O4 nanoparticles prior to HPLC, Separation Science and Technology, 52(2017) 1787–1795.

Sun, et al., Polyaniline/titanium dioxide nanorods functionalized carbon fibers for in-tube solid-phase microextraction of phthalate esters prior to high performance liquid chromatography-diode array detection, J Chromatogr A, 1642(2021) 462003.

Carbon

Anal. Methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yan, Y. Qu, R. Du, W. Zhou, H. Gao, R. Lu, Colorimetric assay based on arginine-functionalized gold nanoparticles for the detection of dibutyl phthalate in Baijiu samples, Analytical Methods, 13(2021) 5179–5186.

Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Xiao, M. Guo, J. Wang, X. Yan, H. Li, C. Qian, et al., Ferrocene-terminated dendrimer functionalized graphene oxide layered sensor toward highly sensitive evaluation of Di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate in liquor samples, Analytica Chimica Acta, 1043(2018) 35–44.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaofang Liu - She is a PhD candidate at Bioengineering College of Chongqing University. Her research interests are focused on the nanotechnology and biomaterials and exploiting their wide applications in bioanalysis.
Jiaxi Deng - She is a master candidate at the School of Bioengineering, Chongqing University. Her research interests include the wide application of nanotechnology in bioanalysis.

Xiaogang Luo - Dr. Xiaogang Luo received his Ph.D degree in Biomedical Engineering from Chongqing University in 2003. He is currently an associate professor of biomedicine at Chongqing University. His current research interests include biomedical signal processing, theory and algorithms for digital medical image processing.

Danqun Huo - Professor Danqun Huo received her PhD degree in biological medical engineering from Chongqing University in 2004 and had once been visited Australia, America, and Italy. She is currently Distinguished Professor of Sichuan University of science and engineering and professor of biomedical science of Chongqing University. Her current research interests include the development of chemical sensors and sensor array, electrochemistry and analytical microsystems.

Changjun Hou - Professor Changjun Hou completed his PhD in biomedical engineering in 2004 at Chongqing University. After a period of research at the University of Illinois at Urbana Champaign as a visiting scholar, he returned to China and got tenure at the college of bioengineering in Chongqing University. In 2010, he became a full professor in biomedical engineering. His scientific interests are the design and construction of sensing systems based on porphyrins and other sensitive materials.
